title: Psoriasis and Sleep: Why Nights Can Be Hard
summary: If psoriasis is disrupting your sleep, you're far from alone. Understanding the link between skin inflammation and sleep is the first step toward better nights.
body:
Why psoriasis and sleep don't always get along

If you find yourself lying awake at night with your skin itching and your mind restless, you are not unusual among people living with psoriasis. Research suggests that between 40 and 80% of people with psoriasis experience significant sleep disruption — making it one of the most commonly reported effects of the condition on daily life.

The reasons are biological. Nocturnal itch tends to intensify at night for two main reasons: the natural drop in body temperature that accompanies sleep triggers an increase in skin blood flow, which heightens itch signals; and the reduced distraction of a quiet, dark room means the brain processes itch more intensely. Separately, the inflammatory signals involved in psoriasis — including IL-6 — are known to interfere with normal sleep architecture, reducing the time spent in the deeper, more restorative stages of sleep.

The sleep-inflammation cycle

There is also a feedback loop worth understanding. Poor sleep elevates cortisol (your stress hormone), and elevated cortisol can increase systemic inflammation — which in turn can worsen your psoriasis and intensify itch the following night. This doesn't make the situation hopeless; it means that even small, consistent improvements to sleep quality can have a meaningful knock-on effect on how you feel during the day.

Practical steps for better sleep

These are evidence-informed strategies that many people with psoriasis find helpful. They complement — rather than replace — your medical care:

  • Keep your bedroom cool — a room temperature of around 16–18°C (60–65°F) supports deeper sleep and reduces the night-time skin temperature rise that triggers itch.
  • Choose breathable bedding — 100% cotton or bamboo sheets are gentle on skin and don't trap heat. Avoid synthetic materials where possible.
  • Apply moisturiser before bed — a thick, fragrance-free emollient applied to affected areas before sleep can reduce overnight skin drying and soften itch intensity through the night.
  • Shower in cool or lukewarm water — a cool shower an hour or two before bed lowers body temperature, which supports sleep onset and can calm itch.
  • Limit alcohol and caffeine in the evening — both disturb sleep quality independently, and alcohol is a recognised psoriasis trigger that directly increases skin inflammation.
  • Maintain a consistent sleep schedule — going to bed and waking at the same time each day strengthens your circadian rhythm, which in turn regulates both sleep quality and immune function.
  • Reduce screen time before bed — blue light from phones and screens delays melatonin release and makes it harder to fall asleep.

When to mention it to your care team

If your sleep is significantly disrupted several nights a week, it's worth raising with your dermatologist or GP. Poor sleep can affect your overall health, your mood, and the course of your psoriasis — and there are clinical approaches that can help. Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) in particular has a strong evidence base and is increasingly available through specialist services or digital programmes.

This content is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for personalised advice from your healthcare team.
